title-group.article-title: Blood pro-thrombotic analytes and platelet activation are associated with post-acute sequelae of COVID-19

abstract.p: Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC), or “long COVID,” describes persistent symptoms following recovery from SARS-CoV-2 infection. Early identification of circulating biomarkers predictive of PASC is critical for prognosis and therapeutic development yet remains poorly defined. To address this gap, we conducted a longitudinal, multi-omics analysis of blood samples from COVID-19 patients (n = 75), stratified by acute disease severity and PASC status. We integrated targeted multiplex assays, untargeted proteomics (LC-MS), and whole blood flow cytometry to define immune and vascular signatures associated with PASC. We found that individuals who went on to develop PASC exhibited a distinct phenotypic signature between 1 and 35 days post-infection, such as significantly elevated plasma Factor-IX, Tissue factor, and tPA, which reflected hyperactivation of immunothrombotic pathways. Similarly, pathway enrichment analysis revealed ongoing neutrophil degranulation, platelet activation, and extracellular matrix remodeling—indicating unresolved inflammation and immunothrombosis which persisted beyond 77 days post-symptom onset. Unlike prior studies using single biomarkers or limited timepoints, our study offers a comprehensive longitudinal analysis combining proteomic and cellular data to define a durable immune-vascular signature specific to PASC. This integrative approach reveals insights into PASC pathogenesis and highlights candidate biomarkers with potential utility in early risk stratification. Our findings underscore the critical role of chronic immune and endothelial dysfunction in long COVID and point toward actionable targets for intervention. This investigation links biorepository human samples with clinical symptoms and lays the foundation for precision diagnostics and therapeutic strategies aimed at improving long-term outcomes in COVID-19 survivors (NCT04603677).

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) is a highly virulent virus responsible for the global Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic, which has caused significant morbidity and mortality worldwide [1–3]. While many individuals recover from acute SARS-CoV-2 infection, a subset develops persistent or newly acquired symptoms that last for months beyond the resolution of the initial illness, a condition now referred to as Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) [4–7]. This condition, often colloquially termed “long COVID,” has emerged as a significant public health concern due to its widespread prevalence and multifaceted clinical manifestations [8–11]. Reports estimate that 35–77% of individuals diagnosed with COVID-19 experience symptoms consistent with PASC at least until 60 days post-infection, with fatigue being the most frequently reported symptom [12–14]. Other commonly reported symptoms include cognitive impairment, dyspnea, and cardiovascular complications. Factors such as female sex, obesity, and pre-existing comorbidities, including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and type 2 diabetes, have been associated with an increased risk of PASC [15–22]. Notably, while severe COVID-19 disease during the acute phase (until 14 days post-infection) has been associated with an increased likelihood of developing PASC [22, 23], less is known about PASC risk in individuals experiencing mild or asymptomatic infection.

SARS-CoV-2 infection is known to drive endothelial damage and micro-clot formation, which has the potential to drive hypoxemia-induced immunothrombosis and subsequent microvascular damage [24–28]. The biological mechanisms underlying PASC are multifactorial and remain an active area of investigation. Potential drivers include persistent viral reservoirs within mucosal tissues, host autoimmunity triggered by molecular mimicry, hyperactivation of immune cells, and dysregulation of the clotting cascade [29]. These processes may collectively contribute to the sustained abnormalities in thrombotic and inflammatory responses observed in PASC. COVID-19 infection has been associated with an increased incidence of cardiovascular diseases including cerebrovascular events, ischemic heart disease, heart failure, myocarditis, and thromboembolic events, even one-year post-infection [30–35].

Identifying plasma biomarkers associated with the host response to SARS-CoV-2 infection and the subsequent development of PASC holds immense potential for advancing our understanding of this complex condition. Additionally, the development of improved prognostic tools based on these biomarkers would empower healthcare providers to make informed decisions, optimize resource allocation, and deliver more effective care, ultimately reducing the burden of PASC on both patients and healthcare systems.

In this study, we leveraged a prospective, longitudinal cohort of adults with COVID-19[37] to interrogate the dynamic interplay between soluble pro-thrombotic and pro-inflammatory mediators in circulation and the subsequent development of PASC. By tracking immune and vascular biomarkers over time, we identified early molecular signatures associated with persistent symptoms and immune dysregulation in PASC. This approach enabled the discovery of predictive biomarkers with potential clinical utility. Our findings not only expand the mechanistic understanding of PASC pathogenesis—particularly the role of chronic immunothrombosis and endothelial dysfunction—but also provide a translational framework to guide precision diagnostics and targeted therapeutic strategies for mitigating long-term COVID-19 complications.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: Adults enrolled in the Northern Colorado COVID-19 biorepository (NoCO CoBIO) registered at ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT04603677) with registration date 2020-10-27, were eligible for inclusion in this longitudinal, observational cohort investigation of pro-thrombotic and inflammatory molecules in blood [36]. Recruitment of newly infected (acute) and previously infected (convalescent) individuals were identified through Colorado State University (CSU) and from the University of Colorado Health (UCHealth) community networks. Inclusion criteria for the biobank were >18 years of age and a positive SARS-CoV-2 polymerase chain reaction test (PCR) result. Participants with a positive SARS-CoV-2 result from an antigen test or a home test kit were not included, and neither were currently pregnant or incarcerated individuals. All participants provided written informed consent prior to the first study visit. NoCO CoBio was conducted in accordance with the Helsinki Declaration and its 2013 amendments. The biorepository was approved by CSU’s Institutional Review Board (IRB; protocol (20–10063 H) and Colorado Multiple IRB (20–6043). The survey used in this study has previously been published elsewhere [36].

A positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR test was used as a diagnostic criterion for SARS-CoV-2 infection. There were 75 participants diagnosed with COVID-19 who completed study visits between July 2020 and April 2021 (Table 1). Study participants consented to complete four biospecimen and clinical visits that included enrollment followed by time points 1, 4, and 6 months after enrollment. An optional study visit one year after enrollment was also provided. At each time point, ~ 45mL of blood was collected via venipuncture for isolation of plasma and PBMCs using BD Cell Preparation Tubes (CPT). Other biospecimens were also collected including nasopharyngeal swabs, saliva, and stool. Cash compensation of twenty-five dollars was given to all participants at each study visit. Each participant’s SARS-CoV-2 PCR + diagnostic result date was utilized for standardizing clinic visit timing during acute (1–20 days post PCR) and for convalescence as 21–44 days, 45–89 days, 90–174 days, 174 + days post PCR.

table-wrap-foot.p: PASC, Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19, defined as a participant reporting any of the following symptoms at least 60 days after testing positive for COVID-19: fatigue, dyspnea, joint pain, chest pain, forgetfulness or absent minded, difficulty concentrating or confusion
BMI Body Mass Index, PMHx Past Medical History, TIIDM Type II Diabetes Mellitus, COPD Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, HTN Hypertension

sec.p:
Participants were categorized by disease severity as per the Yale Quick COVID-19 Severity Index based on number of liters of oxygen they required during acute infection: Mild (0 L supplemental O2), Moderate (1–5 L O2), Severe (>5 L O2) [37]. Information regarding risk factors of disease severity, including age, body mass index (BMI), and comorbidities were collected during clinic visits or through protocol-sanctioned use of hospital electronic medical records. This information was then de-identified and stored in the secure, password-protected Research Electronic Data Capture (REDCap) web application. To identify new or ongoing symptoms, a 70-symptom survey was administered at each study visit. Participants were defined as having post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) if they presented with at least one of the following symptoms for at least 60 days after PCR+: fatigue, dyspnea, joint pain, chest pain, confusion, difficulty concentration, forgetfulness, or absentmindedness (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
Overall summary of the study. Seventy-five participants diagnosed with COVID-19 via detection of SARS-CoV-2 using a PCR test were divided into Mild, Moderate, and Severe groups based on total number of liters of oxygen utilized during acute infection. Blood plasma and platelets were isolated from blood samples taken between July 2020 and April 2021. Cytokine profiling and protein analysis of plasma samples were performed using a 10-plex immunothrombotic ELISA-type assay and proteomics, respectively. Participants with elevated Factor IX, P-selectin, and tPA, in addition to low D-dimer and activated platelets were more likely to develop Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) at least 60 days after infection. NET: neutrophil extracellular traps; tPA: tissue-type plasminogen activator; PSGL-1: P-selectin glycoprotein ligand-1; PCR: polymerase chain reaction

Of the 75 adult participants with COVID-19 enrolled in this study, 35 were hospitalized (Table 1). The mortality rate among all participants and across asymptomatic/mild, moderate, and severe disease groups was 1.3%. The median age was 51 years old, and the average body mass index (BMI) was 30.4. The disease severity was significantly related to sex, and BMI. Those with severe disease were white, male, and those who reported higher BMI at the time of infection (Supplemental Fig. 1). The pre-existing conditions identified in this cohort included Type II Diabetes Mellitus, hypertension, and a history of tobacco use. Participants across all disease severity groups had preexisting conditions and health risk factors consistent with previously published studies of this cohort [36].

sec.title: Blood proteome analysis
sec.p: Blood plasma samples first underwent immunodepletion, protein digestion, tandem mass tag (TMT)-labeling, and basic reverse-phase fractionation, as described in prior studies [38, 39]. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Standard Reference Material (SRM) for Human Plasma (SRM 1950) was included at the beginning and end of each batch for quality control [40].

Immunoaffinity depletion of the 14 most abundant plasma proteins was performed using a Multiple Affinity Removal System (MARS) column (Hu-14 4.6 × 100 mm, Agilent Technologies) coupled with an Agilent 1200 high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system. Flow-through fractions containing low- and mid-abundant proteins were collected and concentrated using 3-kDa molecular mass cutoff Amicon centrifugal filters (Millipore, Burlington, MA) following the manufacturer’s instructions. Depleted proteins were quantified using the bicinchoninic acid (BCA) Protein Assay (Thermo Scientific catalog number, company), denatured in 8 M urea, reduced with dithiothreitol (DTT), alkylated with iodoacetamide, and digested with trypsin after pre-treatment with Lys-C. Following digestion, peptides were desalted and labeled with 16-plex TMT reagents, incorporating an internal control sample in each plex. TMT-labeling reactions were quenched, pooled, and desalted using tC18 SepPak cartridges (Waters, Milford, MA). Samples were fractionated into 96 fractions using high-pH reverse-phase chromatography and concatenated into 24 fractions to enhance peptide identification. Fraction peptide concentrations were adjusted to 0.1 µg/µL for liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis. Immunodepleted and TMT-labeled fractions were analyzed on a NanoAcuity ultra-performance liquid chromatography (UPLC) system coupled with a ThermoFisher Eclipse Tribrid mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). Separation was achieved on a C18 reverse-phase column (70 cm × 75 μm i.d., Phenomenex Jupiter, 3 μm particle size, 300 Å pore size) with a 120-minute gradient. Mass spectrometer settings, as described previously, included precursor ion scanning between 300 and 1800 m/z at 120 K resolution, an automatic gain control (AGC) target of 4E6, and a maximum injection time of 50 ms. Precursor ions were fragmented by higher-energy collisional dissociation (HCD) with a normalized collision energy (NCE) of 32 and quadrupole isolation width of 0.7 m/z. Tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) scans were performed at 50 K resolution with an AGC target of 1.25E5 ions, and dynamic exclusion was enabled for 45 s. Xcalibur spectral RAW files were acquired in centroid mode [41–43].

Protein identifications were made by searching the SwissProt Human Database (July 2021 release) using MS-GF + with mzRefinery for mass spectral recalibration. Search parameters included a parent ion mass tolerance of ± 20 ppm, tryptic digestion allowing up to two missed cleavages, static modifications for cysteine carbamidomethylation (+ 57.0215 Da), TMT labeling on lysine and N-termini (+ 229.1629 Da), and variable modifications for methionine oxidation. Peptide identifications were filtered to a 1% false discovery rate (FDR) at the unique peptide level using a decoy database approach.

Quantitative information for TMT reporter ion intensities was extracted using MASIC. Peptide abundances were normalized to the combined internal standard TMT channel containing an equal concentration of plasma peptides from all patient samples [44].

sec.title: LEGENDplex cytokine bead array (CBA)
sec.p: Following proteome analysis, CBA was performed to quantify the concentrations of upregulated proteins in serum samples. The analysis utilized the BioLegend Human Thrombosis LEGENDplex kit (BioLegend, Cat# 740892, San Diego, CA), following the manufacturer’s protocol. Briefly, concentration standards were prepared using the standard cocktail provided in the kit. For each well of a V-bottom 96-well plate, 25 µL of standards or serum samples, 25 µL of assay buffer, and 25 µL of assay beads were added. The plate was sealed and incubated at room temperature on a shaker at 800 rpm for 2 h. Subsequently, the plate was centrifuged at 500 × g for 5 min at 25 °C, and the supernatant was discarded. Next, 25 µL of detection antibodies were added to each well, and the plate was incubated in the dark on a shaker for 1 h at room temperature. This was followed by the addition of 25 µL of PE-tagged streptavidin to each well, with an additional incubation for 30 min. The plate was then washed, and samples were analyzed using a Cytek Aurora 4-laser flow cytometer. Bead populations were resolved using fluorescence channels YG1-A and R4-A. Data analysis was conducted using LEGENDplex software, which determined the concentration of each analyte in the panel (IL-6, P-selectin, D-Dimer, PSGL-1, tPA, sCD40L, PAI-1, Tissue Factor, Factor IX, and CXCL8) by referencing the standard curve. Assay performance characteristics provided by the manufacturer and validated in our laboratory were as follows: intra-assay CVs < 3% and inter-assay CVs < 6%. Limits of detection were IL-6 = 0.55 pg/mL, P-selectin = 29.5 pg/mL, D-Dimer = 3.5 ng/mL, PSGL-1 = 9.8 pg/mL, tPA = 2.4 pg/mL, sCD40L = 1.22 pg/mL, PAI-1 = 71.8 pg/mL, Tissue Factor = 1.07 pg/mL, Factor IX = 127 pg/mL, CXCL8 = 0.6 pg/mL.

sec.title: Platelets and neutrophils staining by flow cytometry
sec.p: Peripheral whole blood was collected from individuals with and without PASC using 3.2% sodium citrate vacutainer tubes to prevent clotting and minimize ex vivo platelet activation. Samples were processed within two hours of collection to preserve native activation states. For surface staining, 100 µL of whole blood was incubated for 20 min at room temperature in the dark with a panel of fluorochrome-conjugated monoclonal antibodies targeting CD41 (integrin αIIb), CD62P (P-selectin), and PAC-1, which specifically binds to the activated conformation of integrin αIIbβ3. Samples were washed once with PBS to reduce background fluorescence. Flow cytometric acquisition was performed using a BD LSR-II cytometer with standardized voltage settings and compensation controls. Platelets were identified based on low forward scatter (FSC), side scatter (SSC), and CD41 positivity. Platelet activation was quantified by measuring the percentage of CD41⁺ events that co-expressed CD62P and PAC-1. A minimum of 50,000 CD41⁺ events were acquired per sample to ensure statistical robustness. Flow cytometry data were analyzed in FlowJo (v10.8.2), and resulting summary statistics (e.g., percentage of CD41⁺CD62P⁺PAC-1⁺ cells) were exported for statistical analysis and visualization. All data analysis and plotting were performed in R (version 4.3.1).

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: For proteomics data analysis, all data preprocessing was done in R version 4.1.0 with the pmartR package [45]. Proteomics data was normalized to the reference sample by computing the ratio of each sample’s peptide abundances to the respective combined internal standard TMT channel calculated within each plex. Peptide-level data and metabolomics data were log2 transformed and filtered to remove peptides and metabolites without enough observations to conduct at least one statistical comparison of interest. Outlier samples were removed from the dataset based on a robust Mahalanobis distance with a p-value threshold of 0.0001 and visual inspection of principal component analysis and correlation with other samples. For the proteomics data, samples were normalized by the median of rank-invariant peptides at a p-value of 0.15, the method selected by the statistical procedure for analysis of proteomics normalization strategies (SPANS) algorithm for determining the optimal normalization method. Data was then quantified to the protein-level using a median reference-based approach [46–49].

SAS v9.4 (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC) was used for all statistical analyses. All continuous data was evaluated for normality. If normality was met, the categories were compared using a t-test. If normality was not met, the categories were compared using a Wilcoxon 2-sample test. A p-value of 0.05 was used to determine statistical significance.

sec.title: Proteomic signatures reveal differential expression of inflammatory and tissue repair proteins in severe COVID-19 and PASC
sec.p:
The whole proteome of each COVID-19 patient was evaluated, stratifying samples by disease severity (mild, moderate, severe) and PASC status (PASC vs. No PASC), to identify differential protein expression patterns associated with acute and long-term disease outcomes. Figure 2 shows the heatmap representing the z-scored protein abundance of key inflammatory and tissue repair markers across these groups. In severe COVID-19 cases, we observed a significant upregulation of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), such as HMGB1, S10A8, and S10A9, which are known to mediate inflammation and immune cell recruitment during tissue damage [50, 51]. Additionally, proteins involved in tissue remodeling and repair, such as matrix metalloproteinase 9 (MMP9) and the annexin family (ANXA1-ANXA6), were markedly elevated in severe cases [52–54]. In contrast, mild and moderate cases exhibited comparatively lower levels of these proteins. Importantly, patients with PASC displayed a distinct proteomic profile characterized by persistently elevated levels of HMGB1, S10A8, S10A9, and annexins, even when their initial disease severity was not classified as severe. While pro-inflammatory and damage-associated proteins were prominently elevated in both severe cases and PASC samples, the interleukin-6 receptor subunits (IL6RA and IL6RB) exhibited relatively stable expression across all groups.Fig. 2
Heatmap of z-scored protein abundance across COVID-19 severity and PASC status groups. The heatmap illustrates the relative abundance of key inflammatory and tissue repair proteins across disease severity (mild, moderate, severe) and Post Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) status (PASC vs. No PASC). Proteins associated with inflammation (e.g., HMGB1, S10A8, S10A9), tissue remodeling (e.g., MMP9, annexin family: ANXA1-ANXA6), and endothelial or immune activation (e.g., PAI-1, TPA) are shown. Z-scores indicate relative protein abundance, with red representing higher expression and blue representing lower expression. Disease severity is denoted by shades of orange (mild to severe), while PASC status is indicated by blue (No PASC) or teal (PASC) bars above the heatmap. Elevated expression of damage associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and tissue repair proteins in severe cases and PASC patients highlights their roles in inflammation and long-term sequelae of COVID-19

sec.title: Differential proteome and pathway analysis reveals neutrophil degranulation and platelet activation in PASC patients
sec.p:
In cross-sectional comparisons of PASC versus non-PASC (Fig. 3), several inflammatory and thrombo-inflammatory proteins were higher in PASC. Complement C3 (CO3) and BPI remained significantly increased after multiple-testing correction (CO3 FDR ≈ 0.0017; BPI FDR ≈ 0.025), while A2MG (A2M), ANXA3, GABT/ABAT, ATPB/ATP5F1B, and VATG1/ATP6V1G1 also showed significant increase (p < 0.05). These patterns are consistent with heightened innate immune and platelet-linked activity in PASC. To understand how these signals evolve within the syndrome, we evaluated severity- and phase-stratified contrasts inside the PASC cohort (Supplementary Figure S1). In the acute window (1–35 days), severe disease displayed broad up-regulation of innate/acute-phase and complement proteins (VSIG4, SERPINA3/AACT, PSMB9, GPX3, S100A9). During the mid phase (36–76 days), increases shifted toward coagulation/vascular and matrix-remodeling factors (ANXA3, PRSS3, MGP), and by the long phase (> 77 days) differences included proteostasis/RNA-handling proteins (SYNCRIP/HNRNPQ, PSMD3, RBM3), suggesting persistent cellular stress. Guided by these analyte-level findings, we compiled the top proteins up-regulated in PASC (prioritized from Supplementary Figure S1) and performed Reactome over-representation analysis using all quantified proteins as background with BH-FDR control. The resulting pathway map (Fig. 4) was dominated by neutrophil degranulation and platelet activation/signaling/aggregation, with additional enrichment for interleukin and MAPK signaling, RHO GTPase effectors, and extracellular-matrix organization. Taken together, the data indicate that PASC is characterized by sustained innate-immune and thrombo-inflammatory activity with concomitant matrix remodeling and cellular-stress responses.Fig. 3
PASC vs. non-PASC protein expression. Box-and-jitter plots show the distribution of normalized protein expression for the indicated proteins in participants with post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 (PASC, red) versus those without PASC (grey). Each dot is one participant; boxes show the median (center line) and interquartile range (25th–75th percentiles), and whiskers extend to 1.5× the IQR. The y-axis is panel-specific (free scale) to display the full range for each protein. Above each panel, significance is reported from a two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test comparing PASC vs. non-PASC, with Benjamini–Hochberg FDR correction across the plotted proteins; the label gives the raw p and FDR, and stars indicate raw p levels (p < 0.05 = *, p < 0.01 = **, p < 0.001 = ***; “ns” = not significant)Fig. 4
Top 20 enriched pathways from differential expression analysis. Horizontal bars show the number of differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) associated with each pathway (x = Count; y = Pathway description). Bars are ordered by count, and fill color encodes –log10(adjusted p) from the enrichment test (lighter = more significant) How enrichment was done: DEPs were defined as proteins with FDR < 0.05 in any pairwise comparison; the union of these DEPs was used as the input list. Enrichment was performed against Reactome pathway annotations using an over-representation (hypergeometric) test, with the background set equal to all quantified proteins that passed QC in this study. Multiple testing was controlled with Benjamini–Hochberg FDR; plotted colors reflect these adjusted p values. Prominent enriched pathways include neutrophil degranulation, platelet activation/signaling/aggregation, RHO GTPase effectors, interleukin signaling, and MAPK signaling (including MAPK1/3 and RAF/MAP kinase cascades)

sec.title: Biomarker dynamics across disease severity and PASC status
sec.p: After evaluating the whole transcriptome, specific markers associated with platelet activation were assessed using Biolegend LegendPlex CBA. The analysis focused on 10 key biomarkers, including D-Dimer, Factor IX, IL-6, IL-8, P-selectin, Plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 (PAI-1), P-selectin glycoprotein ligand-1 (PSGL-1), sCD40L, Tissue Factor, and Tissue-type plasminogen activator (tPA), to understand their association with disease severity and PASC. Plasma samples were divided between three-time points (1–35 days, 36–76 days, and > 77 days post-symptom onset) from patients stratified by disease severity (mild, moderate, or severe) and PASC status (Fig. 5).

caption.p: Evaluation of serum protein analytes using LegendPlex CBA kit. Concentrations (pg/mL, log10 scale) for various biomarkers measured across different time intervals (1–35 days, 36–76 days, >77 days) and disease severities (Mild, Moderate, Severe). Each panel represents a specific biomarker, including D-Dimer, Factor IX, IL-6, IL-8, P-selectin, plasminogen activator inhibitor -1 (PAI-1), P-selectin glycoprotein ligand-1 (PSGL-1), sCD40L, Tissue Factor, and tissue-type plasminogen activator (tPA). X-axis: Time intervals post-disease onset. Y-axis: Concentration of biomarkers (pg/mL, log10 scale). Colors: Disease severity is indicated by blue (Mild), orange (Moderate), and red (Severe). The figure highlights dynamic changes in biomarker levels over time and their association with disease severity

sec.p: Among assayed biomarkers, Factor-IX, IL-6, Tissue Factor, and tPA were significantly elevated in participants with greater disease severity during the 1–35 day post-PCR + timeframe (Fig. 5). Plasma Factor-IX and IL-6 were significantly elevated in Severe compared to Mild cases of COVID-19, with IL-6 concentrations remaining elevated between days 36–76 in this group. Across all analytes’ measured, plasma concentrations across groups were not significantly different after 77 days post-PCR+. Comparing biomarker levels between patients who go on to develop PASC and those who do not, significantly elevated levels of Factor-IX, Tissue Factor, and tPA were observed in the PASC group during the 1–35 day timeframe (Fig. 6). D-dimer measured between 36 and 76 days post-PCR + also significantly corresponded to PASC development. No difference between analyte concentrations between groups was detected in samples taken after 77 days post-PCR+.

caption.p: Plasma biomarker levels in patients with and without PASC. This figure illustrates the plasma concentrations (pg/mL, log10 scale) of various biomarkers, including D-Dimer, Factor IX, IL-6, IL-8, P-selectin, plasminogen activator inhibitor -1 (PAI-1), P-selectin glycoprotein ligand-1 (PSGL-1), sCD40L, Tissue Factor, and tissue-type plasminogen activator (tPA), stratified by Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) status (No PASC in black and PASC in red) over three time intervals post-disease onset (1–35 days, 36–76 days, and >77 days). The data reveal distinct patterns, with elevated levels of Factor IX, Tissue Factor, and tPA elevated in the early stages of disease (1–35 days) among patients who eventually go on to develop PASC. These findings suggest a potential link between these biomarkers and the development or persistence of PASC, providing insight into the underlying pathophysiology and potential targets for monitoring or intervention

sec.p:
Platelet activation was also evaluated in individuals with and without PASC by staining whole blood with platelet activation markers followed by flow cytometric analysis (Figure-7). Specifically, the expression of CD41, CD62P, and PAC-1—key markers indicative of platelet activation was assessed (Supplementary Figs. 2 and 3). CD41 (integrin αIIb) is a surface glycoprotein involved in platelet aggregation; CD62P (P-selectin) translocates to the membrane upon platelet degranulation; and PAC1 binds specifically to the activated conformation of integrin αIIbβ3, providing a direct readout of activation status. A marked increase in the CD41⁺CD62P⁺PAC1⁺ platelet population was observed in individuals with PASC compared to those without.Fig. 7
Persistent platelet activation in individuals with PASC. Flow cytometry analysis of whole blood was performed to assess platelet activation in individuals with and without Post- Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC). Platelets were stained for CD41 (integrin αIIb), CD62P (P-selectin), and PAC1 (Procaspase-activating compound 1). The frequency of CD41⁺CD62P⁺PAC1⁺ platelets was quantified using FlowJo. Data are displayed as jitter plots overlaid with boxplots, with the y-axis showing the percentage of total CD41⁺CD62P⁺PAC1⁺ cells out of total stained blood cells. Statistical comparison between groups was performed using the two-tailed Mann-Whitney U test. The graph was plotted using R, version 4.3.1

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study offers critical insights into the potential drivers of severe COVID-19 and PASC development by uniquely integrating proteomics, soluble biomarker profiling, and high-dimensional flow cytometry. While previous studies have largely examined acute or convalescent phases in isolation or focused on single biomarker classes, our multi-modal and longitudinal approach captures the temporal evolution of inflammation, coagulopathy, tissue remodeling, and vascular injury across the full disease spectrum. This comprehensive framework reveals how early immune and thrombotic activation and dysregulated endothelial repair processes are related to pathology and chronic symptoms. By mapping immune-vascular signatures throughout the course of disease, we found that early, severe markers of immunothrombosis and endothelial dysfunction are significantly associated with eventual PASC development. These findings not only refine the understanding of COVID-19 pathogenesis but also identify translationally relevant biomarkers and potential therapeutic targets that have been underexplored in prior studies.

In severe COVID-19, we observed a pronounced upregulation of DAMPs, such as HMGB1, S10A8, and S10A9. These molecules amplify inflammation and recruit immune cells, leading to significant tissue injury [55–65]. Simultaneously, elevated levels of tissue remodeling proteins, including MMP9 and annexins, reflect an intense effort to repair damaged tissues [66]. This combination of heightened inflammation and active tissue remodeling underscores the dual-edged nature of the immune response in severe cases: while attempting to control the infection, it inadvertently drives tissue and vascular damage. Such vascular damage may, in turn, promote a hypercoagulable state more conducive to the formation of microthrombi [67]. Indeed, the strong relationship between acutely elevated plasma Tissue Factor, Factor IX and tPA concentrations and eventual PASC development in the present study underscores the potential role of coagulopathy in the pathophysiology chronic COVID-19 symptoms.

Among patients with active PASC, a distinct proteomic profile emerged which was characterized by persistent elevation of inflammatory markers and tissue remodeling proteins irrespective of initial disease severity. Pathway enrichment analyses identified neutrophil degranulation, platelet activation, and extracellular matrix remodeling as central processes, suggesting that unresolved inflammation and chronic vascular damage may also underlie the development of long-term sequelae [68–70]. Patients with PASC have been shown in previous reports to exhibit persistent fibrinolysis-resistant plasma amyloid fibrin microclots and elevated levels of inflammation-associated molecules, such as interleukin-6 (IL-6), C-reactive protein (CRP), and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α)[30, 72–76]. Indeed, persistent proliferation of CD8 + T cells in patients with PASC has previously been reported by this group [76]. Flow cytometry data from the present study demonstrated increased expression of platelet activation markers—CD41, CD62P, and PAC1—in individuals with PASC. CD41 (integrin αIIb) marks platelet aggregation [77, 78], CD62P (P-selectin) reflects degranulation and endothelial activation [79–81], while PAC1 binds the activated form of αIIbβ3 integrin [82–84]. The elevation of CD41⁺CD62P⁺PAC1⁺ platelets in individuals with PASC suggests ongoing platelet priming and a persistent thrombo-inflammatory state, which complements the proteomic findings. Together, these data implicate unresolved coagulopathy and endothelial activation as features of PASC pathogenesis.

Taken together, these findings suggest a prolonged state of immune activation and maladaptive tissue repair, implicating potential progression toward fibrosis and irreversible organ dysfunction. These integrated molecular signatures highlight a pathological failure to resolve inflammation and reestablish vascular homeostasis, highlighting a critical link between immune activation and thrombo-inflammatory processes [85–95]. In the case of PASC, early immunothrombosis hyperactivity may drive vascular damage which sets off a self-reinforcing loop of immune dysregulation, microvascular damage, and impaired tissue regeneration.

Although the present study, alongside others, may be thought to bolster support for therapeutics strategies targeting inflammation and hypercoagulation in patients at risk for PASC, translational studies evaluating the effectiveness of such interventions have yielded mixed results. The anti-inflammatory agent Dexamethasone, for example, was shown to both improve patient outcomes in the setting of acute COVID-19[97] and prevent PASC in patients with moderate and severe disease [97]. Though other reports have identified coagulopathy as an important pathophysiology component of COVID-19 and PASC [28, 72], its precise role as a potential driver of long-term disease is still debated. Following many studies demonstrating no benefit to patients in ambulatory and post-acute settings [98], anticoagulant therapy was similarly shown to be ineffective at attenuating PASC development [99]. Thus, the mechanistic relationship between inflammation, coagulation, and PASC remains somewhat opaque, particularly when a longer time horizon of sequala is considered. This stated, the present study identifies early biomarkers strongly associated with PASC development, as well as provides insights into the immunothrombosis landscape of those experiencing active PASC. Such insight could be leveraged by clinicians to inform patient prognosis and assess longitudinal health risk.

There are several important limitations associated with this study. First, though we utilized participants’ PCR + test date as an objective means of estimating initial infection, it is likely that many individuals had been infected for a number of days prior to testing. Second, as a result of actively recruiting, consenting, and conducting follow-up visits with participants at varied stages of the disease course, there was some heterogeneity in the duration of time between visits among participants. For this reason, we decided to group participants into broad categories depending on how many days post-PCR + samples were collected. Third, because symptom assessments were made only during sample collection to reduce a potential source of bias, it is possible that some symptoms experienced by participants went uncaptured between visits. Similarly, we decided to limit PASC symptoms as most prevalent among patients. Finally, while defining diagnostic cutoff values and performing ROC curve analyses could enhance the clinical translation of our findings, we did not conduct these analyses in the present study because the sample size is insufficient to establish clinically meaningful cutoffs with confidence. Performing such analyses on the current dataset could risk overfitting and potentially misinterpreting the true diagnostic performance of the biomarkers. We recognize the importance of these approaches and are emphasizing that validation in a larger, independent cohort will be a critical next step before clinically relevant cutoff values and robust sensitivity/specificity estimates can be determined.

In conclusion, this study highlights the intricate and longitudinal interplay between inflammation, tissue remodeling, and vascular damage in severe COVID-19 and PASC. Future studies should explore the potential for therapeutic amelioration of PASC disease burden through targeting drivers of early pathology.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors thank Emma McGinnis and Sarah Mast with the Colorado State University Human Performance Clinical Research Laboratory for phlebotomy assistance to the adults enrolled in the NoCo COBIO biorepository. A portion of this research was performed in the Environmental and Molecular Sciences Laboratory (EMSL), a DOE Office of Science User Facility sponsored by the Office of Biological and Environmental Research and located at Pacific Northwest National Laboratory.

sec.title: Clinical trial
sec.p: Participants in the NoCO CoBIO study were enrolled under ClinicalTrials.gov registration number NCT04603677, registered on October 27, 2020.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: KB, SML, NN, BB, MT, and TSD collected blood samples and prepared plasma isolates. TLW assisted with flow cytometry. MHT helped with BSL-3 space and flow cytometry. LW, KB, NN, BB, MT, and TSD performed CBA to assess soluble analytes. Statistical analysis was performed by SR. Proteomics data generation and processing by MAG, KW, VG, LB, and PP. Proteomics statistical analysis and visualization by LB and TSD. Manuscript original draft prepared by LW and TSD. Manuscript reviewed and edited by: TLW and EPR. All authors have read the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was funded by Colorado State University (CSU) and the Poudre Valley Hospital Foundation. Support for personnel, blood collection and processing, materials, reagents, and flow cytometry was provided by E.P.R. in the CSU Department of Environmental and Radiological Health Sciences, in partnership with the Trauma Research Department at the Medical Center of the Rockies (J.D.). Additional funding for clinical recruitment at local hospitals was provided by the University of Colorado Health Northern Foundation. Pilot funding was provided from U.S. Department of Defense’s Joint Program Executive Office (JPEO) COVID-19 FY20/21 CARES Act in collaboration with PNNL-EMSL.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: Please contact the corresponding author for raw data and material.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study was approved by the Colorado State University’s Institutional Review Board (IRB), protocol# 20–10063 H and Colorado Multiple IRB protocol# 20-6043. All participants provided written informed consent prior to the first study visit. The study was conducted in accordance with the Helsinki Declaration and its 2013 amendments.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
